title: 'Never Again' as Negative Theology — How Post-1945 Morality Ran Out of Fuel

source.original: The Post-Christian Condition – The Abrahamic Metacritique (Critique and Digest)

body_html:
After 1945, the West needed to process the catastrophe of the Holocaust — but it could not process it as mere political failure. It required a metaphysical response, and the only mode available was the post-Christian one: negation. Hitler became the secular anti-Christ, the Holocaust the unrepeatable atrocity, and Never Again the negative absolute around which all subsequent moral reasoning organized itself in reverse. From this single boundary, entire moral systems were constructed: human rights, minority protections, the discourse of otherness and victimhood as the only remaining source of moral authority.

The structure is recognizably that of Christian moral theology — the absolute, the unrepeatable event, the obligation that flows from it — operating without Christian ontology. Adorno's insistence that the premier demand upon all education is that Auschwitz not happen again functions as a categorical imperative grounded not in rational universality but in historical catastrophe. The Jew as paradigmatic victim provides the only stable moral ground available to a civilization that had rejected all external grounds.

This negative formation carried genuine moral seriousness for roughly two generations. But negation is not a telos. Not Hitler does not answer the question of what to do, who to be, how to live. A morality built entirely on what must never be repeated offers no positive orientation — no account of the good, only an ever-expanding catalog of the forbidden. The grammar of absolute evil, once untethered from its original referent, inevitably metastasizes.

The result is the condition we now inhabit: everything becomes Hitler, everyone becomes a Nazi, and the original referent is buried under the weight of its own metaphorical extension. The moral currency that once commanded genuine authority is inflated into meaninglessness. What began as a serious reckoning with civilizational evil ends as a rhetorical weapon deployed in every minor political dispute, its power spent precisely because it was never grounded in anything that could sustain it beyond the living memory of the event itself.

This trajectory reveals something important about the post-Christian condition's relationship to moral seriousness. The borrowed capital of Christian moral theology — the sense of absolute obligation, the unrepeatable significance of historical events — can function for a time without its original ground. But it cannot reproduce itself. Each generation inherits a thinner version, until what remains is not moral reasoning but moral posture, the feeling of seriousness substituting for the thing itself.
